summary: Hillel Wayne investigates whether software engineering deserves to be called 'engineering' by interviewing 17 professionals who worked as traditional engineers before becoming software developers. He systematically dismantles common arguments against the title—that software lacks math, high consequences, or licensing—showing that these don't define engineering in other fields either. Of the crossovers interviewed, 15 considered software engineering to be real engineering. Wayne concludes that while not all software developers are engineers, the gap between software development and software engineering is much smaller than in traditional fields, separated by circumstance rather than essence.
key_points: Nobody in the 'is software engineering real engineering' debate has actually worked as a traditional engineer, making their arguments based on stereotypes rather than experience
Software uses discrete math (graph theory, logic, combinatorics) rather than continuous math—different branches, not absence of math
Engineering licensure is a political/social construct that varies by state and country, not an essential quality of engineering itself
15 of 17 crossover engineers interviewed considered software engineering to be real engineering
The gap between 'software craft' and 'software engineering' is much smaller than between electrician and electrical engineer—we're separated by circumstance, not essence
money_quotes: My personal blog has better security than some $100 million mining projects.
Just because there are no integrals doesn't mean we are mathless.
Whether or not we are engineers is irrelevant to whether or not we are good engineers.
Something becomes engineering if enough engineers say it's engineering.
We are separated from engineering by circumstance, not by essence, and we can choose to bridge that gap at will.
If chemical engineering started now, people would refuse to call it engineering. And they'd be wrong to refuse.
key_insight: The debate over whether software engineering is 'real' engineering has been conducted entirely by people who've never worked as traditional engineers; those who have crossed over from traditional engineering mostly say yes, it counts.
